stem: Use polar coordinates to evaluate \( \iint_D [[c]](x^2+y^2)\,dA \), where \(D\) is the disk \( x^2 + y^2 \le [[R2]] \).
solution: In polar coordinates \(x^2+y^2=r^2\) and \(dA = r\,dr\,d\theta\), so \( \iint_D [[c]](x^2+y^2)\,dA = \int_{0}^{2\pi}\int_{0}^{[[R]]} [[c]]\,r^2 \cdot r\,dr\,d\theta = \int_{0}^{2\pi}\int_{0}^{[[R]]} [[c]]\,r^3\,dr\,d\theta = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the Jacobian factor \(r\), integrating \([[c]]r^2\) instead of \([[c]]r^3\).
This uses the plain area of the disk times \([[c]]\) rather than integrating \(x^2+y^2\).
This drops the factor of \(1/2\) from \(\int_0^{[[R]]} r^3\,dr = [[R]]^4/4\).
This adds a stray multiple of \(\pi\) to the correct value.
This subtracts a stray multiple of \(\pi\) from the correct value.

stem: Use polar coordinates to evaluate \( \iint_D [[c]](x^2+y^2)\,dA \), where \(D\) is the annulus \( [[a]]^2 \le x^2+y^2 \le [[b]]^2 \).
solution: With \(dA = r\,dr\,d\theta\), the integral is \( \int_{0}^{2\pi}\int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} [[c]]r^3\,dr\,d\theta = 2\pi[[c]]\cdot\frac{[[b]]^4-[[a]]^4}{4} = [[answer]] \); note the inner radius \([[a]]\) is NOT \(0\), since \(D\) is a ring, not a full disk.
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by \(4\) in the antiderivative of \(r^3\).
This uses the area formula \(\pi(b^2-a^2)\) instead of integrating \(r^3\).
This mistakenly uses the antiderivative of \(r^2\) (giving \(r^3/3\)) instead of \(r^3\).
This adds a stray multiple of \(\pi\) to the correct value.
This subtracts a stray multiple of \(\pi\) from the correct value.

stem: Use polar coordinates to evaluate \( \iint_D [[c]]x^2\,dA \), where \(D\) is the upper half-disk \( x^2+y^2 \le [[R2]],\ y \ge 0 \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \(\int_0^\pi\cos^2\theta\,d\theta = \pi\) instead of the correct value \(\pi/2\).
This drops a factor of \(4\) from the radial antiderivative \(r^4/4\).
This mishandles the angular integral, using \(2\pi/4\) in place of \(\pi/2\).
This adds a stray multiple of \(\pi\) to the correct value.
This subtracts a stray multiple of \(\pi\) from the correct value.

stem: Convert to polar coordinates and evaluate \( \int_{0}^{[[R]]} \int_{0}^{\sqrt{[[R]]^2-x^2}} [[c]]\sqrt{x^2+y^2}\,dy\,dx \).
solution: The region \(0\le x\le [[R]]\), \(0\le y\le\sqrt{[[R]]^2-x^2}\) is the quarter-disk of radius \([[R]]\) in the first quadrant, and \(\sqrt{x^2+y^2}=r\). So the integral becomes \( \int_{0}^{\pi/2}\int_{0}^{[[R]]} [[c]]\,r\cdot r\,dr\,d\theta = [[c]]\frac{\pi}{2}\cdot\frac{[[R]]^3}{3} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the full angular sweep \(2\pi\) instead of the quarter-disk's \(\pi/2\).
This uses a half-disk's angular sweep \(\pi\) instead of the quarter-disk's \(\pi/2\).
This uses angular sweep \(2\pi/3\)-style bookkeeping, an arithmetic slip in combining the constants.
This adds a stray multiple of \(\pi\) to the correct value.
This subtracts a stray multiple of \(\pi\) from the correct value.

stem: The circle \( x^2+y^2 = [[a]]\cdot 2x \) has polar equation \( r = 2[[a]]\cos\theta \). Using this, find the area enclosed by the circle by evaluating \( \iint_D 1\,dA \) in polar coordinates.
solution: The area is \( \int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2}\int_{0}^{2[[a]]\cos\theta} r\,dr\,d\theta = \int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2} 2[[a]]^2\cos^2\theta\,d\theta = 2[[a]]^2\cdot\frac{\pi}{2} = [[answer]] \), matching the known area \(\pi[[a]]^2\) of a circle of radius \([[a]]\).
distractor_rationales: This doubles the correct area, as if the angular range were \(-\pi\) to \(\pi\) with no adjustment.
This halves the correct area, mishandling the \(\cos^2\theta\) integral.
This uses radius \(2[[a]]\) instead of the true radius \([[a]]\) of the circle.
This adds a stray multiple of \(\pi\) to the correct value.
This subtracts a stray multiple of \(\pi\) from the correct value.
